title: The car lot
question: {local_first_name} shops for a used car. The dealer opens at {currency_amount_large} € and {local_first_name} fixates on beating the sticker by only {currency_amount_small} €. What should {local_first_name} do first?

options.label: A
options.text: Offer exactly {currency_amount_small} € below the sticker

options.label: B
options.text: Gather independent market comps, then negotiate from data

options.label: C
options.text: Trust the sticker as fair because professionals set it

options.label: D
options.text: Avoid research for {timeframe_days} days to save time

explanation: The sticker is a classic anchor; unbiased choice is independent valuation before any number is treated as meaningful (anchoring bias).

title: Salary talk
question: {local_first_name} interviews for a role. The recruiter says the band tops out near {currency_amount_large} € and mentions a {percentage}% raise cap from the prior hire. {local_first_name} now mentally anchors to that cap. What is the sound approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Treat the band as the hard ceiling without external data

options.label: B
options.text: Research market wages for the role and city before negotiating

options.label: C
options.text: Ask for exactly {percentage}% more than the prior hire

options.label: D
options.text: Accept the first number to seem cooperative

explanation: Recruiter figures anchor expectations; market data breaks the anchor (anchoring bias).

title: Project deadline
question: A client suggests a {timeframe_days}-day sprint because last year a similar job finished that fast. The team now plans resources around that timeline even though scope doubled. What should the lead do?

options.label: A
options.text: Keep the {timeframe_days}-day plan to match history

options.label: B
options.text: Re-estimate from tasks and risk, ignoring the old timeline as an anchor

options.label: C
options.text: Promise {percentage}% buffer verbally but keep the old dates internally

options.label: D
options.text: Skip estimation to avoid conflict

explanation: Past timelines anchor planning; rational PMs recompute from scope (anchoring bias).

title: Clinic quote
question: {local_first_name} needs a procedure. Clinic A quotes {currency_amount_large} € first; Clinic B quotes {currency_amount_small} €. {local_first_name} assumes Clinic B must be cutting corners. What is the balanced move?

options.label: A
options.text: Pick Clinic A solely because the higher quote feels safer

options.label: B
options.text: Compare credentials, outcomes, and inclusions independent of order of quotes

options.label: C
options.text: Split the difference between {currency_amount_small} and {currency_amount_large}

options.label: D
options.text: Avoid both because prices differ

explanation: First or highest prices anchor quality judgments; evidence should drive the choice (anchoring).

title: Coffee donation
question: A charity tablet suggests gifts of {currency_amount_large} €, {currency_amount_small} €, or custom. Most pick {currency_amount_small} € because it looks modest next to {currency_amount_large} €. What best counters anchoring here?

options.label: A
options.text: Always pick the middle number shown

options.label: B
options.text: Decide based on your budget and impact research, not the preset amounts

options.label: C
options.text: Match the highest suggestion to avoid guilt

options.label: D
options.text: Donate zero because menus manipulate people

explanation: Suggested amounts anchor generosity; a planned donation avoids the nudge (anchoring bias).

title: Vaccine leaflet
question: A clinic flyer says the vaccine is {percentage}% effective. Another flyer for the same vaccine says {local_first_name} faces a small chance of illness in the next {timeframe_days} days without it. Same facts, different frames. Which mindset helps a rational choice?

options.label: A
options.text: Pick based on whichever flyer you saw first

options.label: B
options.text: Translate both frames into consistent metrics like absolute risk reduction

options.label: C
options.text: Assume positive framing is always manipulative

options.label: D
options.text: Ignore numbers and follow social media sentiment

explanation: Framing bias is neutralized by converting narratives into comparable statistics.

title: Subscription trial
question: A streamer offers a trial for {currency_amount_small} € today or {currency_amount_large} € after. {local_first_name} focuses on how cheap today feels. What is the clearest next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Buy because today is cheap without reading renewal terms

options.label: B
options.text: Annualize total cost and cancellation rules before deciding

options.label: C
options.text: Assume {currency_amount_large} € will never be charged

options.label: D
options.text: Pick randomly between offers

explanation: Highlighting a small today fee hides long-term cost; normalize to totals (framing effect).

title: Investment slide
question: Slide A says an index rose {percentage}% last quarter. Slide B shows the same move wiped {percentage}% off a large notional portfolio. Executives react oppositely to the slides. What should {local_first_name} the analyst do?

options.label: A
options.text: Use whichever slide leadership prefers

options.label: B
options.text: Present both percent and absolute figures with the same baseline

options.label: C
options.text: Drop numbers entirely to avoid bias

options.label: D
options.text: Show only the positive slide

explanation: Identical performance looks different by frame; pair metrics (framing effect).

title: Workout app
question: App copy says you will lose habits in {timeframe_days} days if you skip once. Another screen says most users stay consistent with gentle reminders. How should {local_first_name} decide whether to pay for premium?

options.label: A
options.text: Pay immediately to avoid the loss frame

options.label: B
options.text: Evaluate features versus free alternatives independent of fear copy

options.label: C
options.text: Assume both frames are equally scientific

options.label: D
options.text: Never pay because scare tactics are unfair

explanation: Loss-framed marketing exploits feelings; judge product value on needs (framing).

title: Bus reliability
question: The operator advertises {percentage}% on-time trips versus a rival citing only {currency_amount_small} major delays per month. Riders pick the operator with nicer percentages, though the delay counts differ in scale. What is rational?

options.label: A
options.text: Pick the highest percentage headline

options.label: B
options.text: Normalize to the same unit like expected delay minutes per ride

options.label: C
options.text: Avoid math entirely

options.label: D
options.text: Follow whichever brand advertises loudest

explanation: Competing frames require converting to common units before comparing (framing effect).

title: Bonus clawback
question: {local_first_name} can keep a sure {currency_amount_small} € bonus or risk it for a coin flip doubling to {currency_amount_large} € on heads. The EV matches, yet {local_first_name} hates losing the sure amount. What reflects unbiased reasoning?

options.label: A
options.text: Always keep the sure {currency_amount_small} € because losses hurt more

options.label: B
options.text: Weigh the utility of both outcomes; equal EV means indifference for risk-neutral agents

options.label: C
options.text: Reject both because any risk is bad

options.label: D
options.text: Flip a coin emotionally

explanation: Loss aversion overweights avoiding a loss versus gaining the same amount; EV analysis helps.

title: Medical test
question: A screening has a {percentage}% false alarm rate but catches serious issues early. {local_first_name} skips it fearing bad news more than valuing information. What is the balanced view?

options.label: A
options.text: Skip testing entirely to avoid anxiety

options.label: B
options.text: Model likelihoods with a clinician and decide given priors and costs

options.label: C
options.text: Assume {percentage}% always means useless

options.label: D
options.text: Test daily to eliminate uncertainty

explanation: Fearing diagnosis more than information reflects loss aversion; expect value of info matters.

title: Insurance rider
question: {local_first_name} pays extra to avoid a small chance of paying {currency_amount_large} € out of pocket next year. The rider costs more than its expected loss, but fear of a bill dominates. What is prudent?

options.label: A
options.text: Buy every rider offered

options.label: B
options.text: Compare premium to expected payout using real probabilities

options.label: C
options.text: Skip all insurance because fear is irrational

options.label: D
options.text: Choose based on advertising fear alone

explanation: Loss aversion inflates willingness to overpay for thin coverage; compare price to EV.

title: Stock stop-loss
question: {local_first_name} refuses to sell a declining stock because locking in a {percentage}% paper loss feels worse than hoping it rebounds. What aligns with rational portfolio rules?

options.label: A
options.text: Hold forever to avoid realizing loss

options.label: B
options.text: Re-evaluate thesis and diversification; cut if fundamentals changed

options.label: C
options.text: Sell immediately on any down day

options.label: D
options.text: Double down solely to avoid regret

explanation: Holding losers to dodge realized pain is loss aversion; decisions should follow valuation.

title: Lunch credits
question: Cafeteria credits worth {currency_amount_small} € expire tonight. {local_first_name} buys extra dessert to not waste them, adding calories worthless otherwise. What is smarter?

options.label: A
options.text: Overconsume because wasting credits feels unbearable

options.label: B
options.text: Treat spent credits as sunk; choose food for health now

options.label: C
options.text: Hoard credits though they expire

options.label: D
options.text: Buy random items to maximize spending

explanation: Fear of 'losing' credit makes people overpay in calories—loss aversion toward waste.

title: Politics feed
question: {local_first_name} believes a policy works and only reads outlets that report {percentage}% positive outcomes, dismissing critiques as noise. What improves reasoning?

options.label: A
options.text: Mute opposing views to stay calm

options.label: B
options.text: Actively seek disconfirming evidence and best opposing arguments

options.label: C
options.text: Trust any headline matching prior beliefs

options.label: D
options.text: Assume all media is equally biased so ignore it all

explanation: Confirmation bias filters evidence; steelmanning the other side counteracts it.

title: Performance review
question: A manager remembers the last {timeframe_days} days of good work and ignores earlier misses when rating {local_first_name}. What process reduces bias?

options.label: A
options.text: Rely on latest impressions only

options.label: B
options.text: Use structured criteria and notes across the whole cycle

options.label: C
options.text: Ask peers who already agree with you

options.label: D
options.text: Rate entirely from memory

explanation: Cherry-picking recent supportive facts confirms preconceptions; structure counters it.

title: Side hustle
question: {local_first_name} invested {currency_amount_small} € and ignores red flags because early customers praised the idea. Which step is most objective?

options.label: A
options.text: Collect only positive testimonials

options.label: B
options.text: Interview churned users and test falsifying hypotheses

options.label: C
options.text: Assume praise equals product-market fit

options.label: D
options.text: Quit because doubt feels uncomfortable

explanation: Early praise confirms hope; hunting falsifying data resists confirmation bias.

title: Health headline
question: A study with {percentage}% significance is shared widely because it matches what {local_first_name} already eats. What should they verify?

options.label: A
options.text: Adopt the diet immediately

options.label: B
options.text: Read methods, sample size, replication, and conflicts of interest

options.label: C
options.text: Dismiss if social media likes are high

options.label: D
options.text: Trust because the number is above half

explanation: Alignment with prior belief raises confirmation risk; methodology checks matter.

title: Hiring loop
question: {local_first_name} prefers a candidate who shares hobby trivia and downplays structured scores. What improves fairness?

options.label: A
options.text: Hire on vibe

options.label: B
options.text: Weight blinded work samples and consistent rubrics higher

options.label: C
options.text: Ask only friendly references supplied by the candidate

options.label: D
options.text: Assume chemistry predicts performance

explanation: Affinity confirms likability hypotheses; structured evidence reduces confirmation bias.

title: Night classes
question: {local_first_name} spent {currency_amount_large} € and {timeframe_days} days on a certificate that no longer fits career goals. Continuing feels mandatory. What is rational?

options.label: A
options.text: Finish solely because of past spend

options.label: B
options.text: Compare future value of finishing versus switching paths, ignoring spent money

options.label: C
options.text: Quit immediately without analysis

options.label: D
options.text: Double time to justify the old spend faster

explanation: Past tuition is sunk; only forward costs and benefits should decide (sunk cost fallacy).

title: Bad movie
question: {local_first_name} already paid {currency_amount_small} € for a dull film ticket and stays to the end to not waste it. What reflects economic reasoning?

options.label: A
options.text: Stay because the ticket money would be 'lost' otherwise

options.label: B
options.text: Leave if time is better elsewhere; ticket cost is sunk

options.label: C
options.text: Sleep in the seat to maximize seat usage

options.label: D
options.text: Force friends to stay too

explanation: Ticket price is sunk; staying wastes additional time.

title: Failing project
question: A project consumed {percentage}% of budget with little traction. Leaders push on to salvage pride. What should planning prioritize?

options.label: A
options.text: Continue to recover sunk budget

options.label: B
options.text: Estimate incremental return versus pivoting; ignore spent funds

options.label: C
options.text: Hide metrics to protect morale

options.label: D
options.text: Fire everyone immediately

explanation: Throwing good money after bad honors sunk costs; marginal analysis guides pivots.

title: Gym membership
question: {local_first_name} pays monthly but has not trained in {timeframe_days} days, yet renews because each visit should cost under {currency_amount_small} € if they return. What is clear thinking?

options.label: A
options.text: Renew to amortize past misses

options.label: B
options.text: Decide renewal from expected future use, not past underuse

options.label: C
options.text: Cancel out of guilt without a plan

options.label: D
options.text: Buy a yearly lock-in to force discipline

explanation: Averaging past payments confuses sunk membership with future motivation.

title: Vendor contract
question: The team picked a vendor after {currency_amount_large} € in integration. New benchmarks show cheaper options. Leadership hesitates because switching wastes the integration spend. What is best?

options.label: A
options.text: Stay to honor past integration expense

options.label: B
options.text: Model switching costs versus lifetime savings including migration

options.label: C
options.text: Never revisit vendor decisions

options.label: D
options.text: Blame procurement and stall

explanation: Integration spend is sunk; compare forward totals including cutover costs.
